C[RNGS: where in Germany are you? i just spent the last 4 nights in Frankfurt, was very nice
C[Thomas: I´m sorry that I missed your question. I live 400 km away from Frankfurt. Stopover for 4 days - was it for private reasons?
C[RNGS: no worries. business the week before and week after, easier to stay the weekend than fly back, and was connecting thru frankfurt
C[Thomas: Connecting flights via Frankfurt may indicate long-distance flights home. Which language is your mother tongue?
RNGS: Klingon. But my 2nd language is english. I live in San Francisco, so mostly fly United which has a lot of connections through Frankfurt.

C[Thomas: My hunch is that some cineasts here use Klingon language from time to time. Living in SF and playing go arouses suspicion that you work in the IT field. But it doesn´t explain biz flights around the world.
Thomas: Last time I was in SF was 2011 when I met an international research group.

C[RNGS: what kind of research do you do? i work with technology companies but as an investment banker, am working with a client in Romania so was visiting Bucharest, and then attended a conference at Ruschlikon in Switzerland whoch was buetiful, but for a californian already a bit cold. i started playing Go in high school, got interested in it from a book. some go players here locally but not as many as you might think. the game with a hugh percentage of techies playing is Ingress - have you heard of that?

Thomas: I live in a rural area, in which a strong Wi-Fi connection is still not standard. We work on that :-) I have never heard of Ingress before, but learned a strong internet connection would be helpful.

Thomas: Clinical research for a pharmaceutical company is my daily business - for more than 2 decades. At university I heard of go, bought some books, but didn´t understand the rules. Internet was still not invented. Only twelve years ago I joined ""local"" go clubs, but meanwhile I have a lack of time for playing face-to-face.
C[RNGS: apparently ingress players in rural areas build ""boosters"" to get a better mobile signal. you dont need wifi but do need a mobile signal. it is a very addictive game.

RNGS: i also learned go beforeinternet so played for many years just in the real world. now i pretty much only play on OGS, when i do go to a go club it seems unusual to play in real life. the internet changes many things...
C[Thomas: Well put. My go board went dusty, but turn-based go server like OGS are a very good compromise.
C[RNGS: nicely played, thanks for the game
Thomas: Frankly said, at first I thought that I have gambled it away. Thanks, too.

Thomas: Nevertheless you won a game, that counts. Frankly said, for live games I have a lack of time - and concentration. I tried on KGS as guest some time ago, but lost and lost, often due to silly mistakes. It irritates me, that my strength differs so significantly as a function of time. As a consequence, a couple of turn-based games on several days of a week is a suitable go dosis.

C[Thomas: We often claim to elect a program and not a single candidate. In fact we prefer an excellent character who should give the rules for four years. After the election several combinations/coalitions of two or three parties will be checked who will have the majority in the parliament. The current incumbent was a Messiah, at least in the European´s eye. It appears he was blocked where ever possible. His successor will be f or m, but from my perspective it will be more significant, who are the playmakers in the background and whether the defeated party will be a sore looser or not. I am afraid there will be a harsh wind blowing soon from the west.

Thomas: At least you voted. A German citizen is autmatically registered and may vote on the polling day or may refuse any vote. Both ways are allowed or democratic basics. These abstentions are protest votes and reached 30% in our last Chancellor election and even 60% in election for the EU. Maybe this protest is not helpful, but a basic right. The winning party reached only 28% of the population - adjusted as the relative majority. The US system is different. In four hours - at 1 am - we will learn the first prelim results. The Western part of Europe keeps the fingers crossed more for the ""mom"", the Eastern part more for the ""dad"". So, every new CIC will be welcomed depending on the location :-) All pray.

C[Thomas: You can say, this is the dilemma of democracy. The majority is a relative figure and not obvious or desired candidate. The world is not like journalists draw us. And how should one cope with the reality that deeply differs from the own sight. People in the rural area might be more conservative, older and have to fear for their jobs or don´t have any. People living in the urban area are younger, well-educated, support most often left-wing politics and think of the next career move. It is impossible to assume that one size of politics size fits all. US citizens belonging to the latter group life on the west east coasts the others rather in the middle countries. The new POTUS promised a lot of and have to provide results. But HRC did not promise the contrary, she would have had to provide the same results. An important question is: Is there still one nation or are there 50 different ""countries"".

Thomas: The illegal immigration was a big issue last year in Europe. Most often war refugees and economic migrants flooded Europe. A lot of families with need of care came to Europe in order to move to Germany, together with Sweden a country who welcomed war refugees even if they were unregistered. It was a very big challenge and a lot of people took pain willingly and self-sacrificingly in order just to help. People who warned of potential criminals or put asyl for economic migrants (predominantly young, healthy men) in question were criticized as far-wright. For me it comes up short that left wing supporters are always do-gooders and all the others the disputable ones on the wrong course. You have to listen to both sides although I do not accept right or left extremism. We often have an indexing of academics as the morally superior ones and non-academics as the uninformed. The world isn´t that simpel. A lot of politicians forgot to listen. And the ones who can will be successful.

C[Misawa: All very well said and well met. I think the urban / rural dynamic is a very real one and the source of a lot of this conflict. Whether states are ""red"" or ""blue"" - the cities are still often democrat and progressive leaning and the rural areas are more conservative. Politically there are many ways to look at this. But I think the fact that Trump's number's would not move no matter what horrible thing he said is really telling. He tapped into a racial backlash against Obama who represents the rise of the ""other"" people in this country - meaning anyone who isn't a white Christian. They do not want to share power, money, jobs, or be in a community that is not like the white dominated culture they remember. This will probably be the last time they are able to pull this off. To ignore the perceived racial threat by our changing demographics to white supremacy is to misunderstand what just happened. Any other explanation is just trying to sugar coat the ugly truth.

C[Thomas: Of course, your statement is thought-provoking and I don´t want to use the typical counter-argument: yes, but ...
Thomas: Of course, your statement is thought-provoking and I don´t want to use the typical counter-argument: yes, but ...

Thomas: Here a widespread frustration among the (white is ancillary) working class, a group that has been left behind, raised an immigration issue. Some (new) parties insinuated that those left-behinds are sort of superior and fueled a competitiveness about jobs. For a long time no money was available. Instead all was explained with empty cashpoints and a mandatory need to economize everything. Then lots of migrants flooded Europe and suddenly a horn of plenty flushed sums of money. Left-behinds (incl. fellow countrymen with a migration background) who mourned, were critized as right-wing sympathizers. Is it crude to ask whether the cake is big enough to share power, money, jobs. This is when hate thrives - hate for immigrants or even the media and hate for anything that seems elitist or is simply different. This does not justify the hate, it may simply explain it. On the agenda ideas like a higher minimum wage and investments in infrastructure and education should be just to increase to cake. Maybe, these ideas have already become cliches.

Thomas: I guess this US election was about the impotent and about power. Seemingly, the army of the powerless is/was so enormous and not only in the rost belt. The Reps sensed that the left-behinds yearned for strength. But now they need ideas they don´t have, but they have to pull them like a rabbit out of a hat. Soon, otherwise the risks are high. Politicians and anyone involved in education should try to reestablish ties to those who feel left behind and strive to achieve true equal opportunity. From my perspective this isn´t a race issue, but it´s easy for you to talk.

C[Misawa: It isn't any one thing - but a few things. It is about those who feel left behind. But you are right to see that they have been conned. It is also about race. Trump aggressively played on racial tensions in this country. It started before he even ran for president in his attempts to de-legitamize Obama's presidency. He ran against Obama more than he ran against Hillary. His support from white supremacist groups is something he never distanced himself from - and has now appointed as one of his top aides someone who ran a media company that catered to the crowd. Reaching out to those who are different from you is difficult as it is. I see progressives make that attempt - I don't see reactionaries even looking that direction. That is the sad part. It takes two willing partners to find unity. As upset as I am - I would try to find common ground if I believed there was any interest in it on the other side.

C[Thomas: We have 9 public holidays in our federal state, other like (Catholic) Bavaria has 12 days extra, weekend days included. If there is a national holiday on a Thursday, a lot of companies offer a day off also on Friday, called a bridge day. In May we often have only 19 working days. My 8 holidays in May result in 16 days w/o office.

C[Thomas: With US-colleagues we are often  in the 2nd email on first-name terms. When I get an email they address me as ""Dear Thomas,"" or ""Thomas:"". We would never answer with ""John:"", because it appears that the sender shouts. Only ""Dear John,"" is regarded as polite. But it is German thinking. How do you think about ""John:""?

C[Misawa: ""Dear"" is very formal, and can be considered intimate as in ""my dearest."" It is losing favor here in a business setting, though it is still used. It is acceptable, and more common to just start with a name. It isn't considered shouting. I even get emails from colleagues where they consider the email address as the salutation and just start talking. I don't like that because you aren't 100% sure they know who they are talking to you. What if they accidentally sent it to you? Without a salutation of some kind you aren't always sure. You can also use ""Hi John,"" that is friendly and casual. If you are familiar with someone, Hi isn't too casual and acceptable business language.

C[Thomas: That is very interesting. Currently I spend most of my office time for our US partner. The subject is a risk assessment of their pipeline opportunities. We correspond on a regular basis and it is a real chance to learn about different views on the same item. Salutation form in email is one (minor) thing, an other US preference for abbreviations. It seems you can write a medical text book without any term in full. My counterpart are guys living in CA who have money and are ready to spend a lot of in clinical trials. Completely new world. However, a great deal of extra work what seems to be 24/7.

C[Misawa: Sorry, I missed what you wrote. When I'm on my phone I can't always notice the comments. That sounds interesting- and exhausting. Pipeline? Are they planning to funnel drug trials through your company? Sounds like a lot of work for you. How awesome. Abbreviations!! Try reading your kids texts to their friends. I swear it is a code to keep us out of their conversation.

C[Thomas: Here it is at the moment morally difficult to justify any vacation abroad. Every day we receive news about new hotspots which do not result in regional lockdowns, but more tests and the risk of quarantine for 14 days. It may be surprise, because Germany could be regarded as an area with controlled corona cases. The subject still dominates all news, if there is not an outrage over #45.
Thomas: Did this situation have an impact on your trip and the ability to relax?

C[Misawa: It was an issue at times. Amethel would enter all hotel rooms before us and use our own clearers to sanitize the room. But most of the trip we were in a car alone, or out in rural locations mostly alone. We spent about a half hour passing through southern rural Idaho - that was weird. They don't seem to be aware there is a pandemic there. We were the only people wearing masks. Fortunately we were only there briefly.

Misawa: But during the times we were in an urban environment or with friends - we still stayed apart. Didn't hug my relatives when I saw them. We only visited them outside and stayed apart with masks on. So it was weird - but then what isn't anymore?
Misawa: Most of the time - we were alone with just us in the car or out in nature. Rarely other people - or if there were others, easy to stay away from them.

Misawa: Bigger issue for relaxing was all the driving. And the fact that restaurants are closed. Usually it is nice to take a break from being in the car and go in and eat somewhere. Most of the time we ate our own food in the hotel, or did drive take-out and ate that in the car. Not near as nice.
C[Thomas: This is a report for the annals. Hopefully, later no one will believe that this really happened. Now, every detail is an essential option.
Thomas: Well, the allowed maximum speed - I remember 55 mph on highways - is a battle against spontaneous sleep. Crazy peolple here think, even 155 mph in a car (not a plane) is still okay.
Misawa: Yeah, it was an odd traveling during a pandemic. It definitely took some planning and preparation - and just awareness. If some place looked crowded - we just moved on.

Misawa: We had so many long straight roads - I used the cruise control to keep me from going above 80-85 mph. It was really easy to do and not notice. But we were driving a big Chevy Suburban - it was nice but did not stop quickly.

Misawa: Most speed limits top out at 70. So I was being a bit of an outlaw. That said, people in actual fast cars often passed me like I was going slow. Most of the time I tried to keep it closer to 75. But when it is late, and the road is straight, and no one is around - 85 was really easy to do.

C[Thomas: Just told in confidence: Within a distance of <2m transmission is easy. The infect can range from nothing to a could or a letal fibrosis/pneumonia. No sign/symptom is predictive enough. Sopas or dishwashing detergents (frequent hand washing) destroy the virus at once. Frequent drinking may help to swallow an inhaled viruses in the mouth and the gastric juices destroy the intruders.

Thomas: In medicine the signal to noise ratio is replaced by terms like sensitivity and specifity or the question how often a test is right positive or right negativ. It is never 100% for both sides. The next term is the positive predictive value, how many patients with a positive lab test are really ill. WHO assumes that in low prevalence areas the positive predictive value might be lower. It is only an academic view, but statistics can drive you crazy.

Thomas: That is the reason why treating patients is often regarded as art.
Thomas: But I admit, this approach is not suitable for a rapid decision for hospital staff. But rapid and reliable single tests are window dressing.
Thomas: COVID prefers the lower respiratory tract, the virus load in nasal swaps then is often far too low, but PCR answers with a false negative result, which means treacherous confidence.
Thomas: That is the blind spot of so much lab tests and one the reason why dark figures of COVID are suspected to be high.

Thomas: Efficacy of two Pfizer shots is up to 95%. Vaccine efficacy is generally reported as a relative risk reduction. Do you know your individual risk of being infected and becoming ill with COVID-19 that could be reduced by 95%? In times of high infection rates every relative change is ""significant"". But how is it in times of low infection rates?

Thomas: Absolute risk reduction, which is the difference between attack rates with and without a vaccine, tends to be ignored because they give a much less impressive effect size than RRRs: 0.84% for the Pfizer vaccines. The number needed to vaccinate to prevent one more case of COVID-19 is 119 for the Pfizer shots. Did you expect these figures?

Thomas: Only 1,131 teenagers had been tested with Pfizer in a pivotal study and the follow-up phase for side effects was limited to 1 or 2 months. Enough for robust recommendations? The German pediatric society said no.
Thomas: Despite all that, I am very happy that my family will have been completely vaccinated until August, both daughters included. Especially because of raising variants of concern (VOC).
C[Misawa: I'm trying to see if I understand what you are saying: The risk reduction percentage is relative to the amount of cases?

Thomas: We cannot translate Dems or Reps into our system, they cannot be allocated to one of our major parties. Somewhere I read, Dems and Reps see themselves as a ""big tent“, which fits your description. With their programs, they want to appeal to as many sections of the population as possible, although you may devide them into left or right. And because there are only two parties with a chance, protests from the voters cannot be expressed in the rise of certain parties. Instead, more radical candidates assert themselves within the party (= #45). Carefully assumed, it appears that the tragic thing about a two-party system is that every side describes the other (adverse) side in extreme categories and any middle-class center seems abolished.

Thomas: In our parliament we have 7 different parties from left to right and all claim to represent the middle. In the last years it forced two major parties to find a compromise in order to form the government. In future we will need more than two. That is an advantage and disadvantage at the same time. But we are accustomed to this diversity. Our daily battle are alleged salary gaps between men and women. It´s here a very touchy subject.

C[thomas: I see, you have European roots.
Interestingly, our ancestors are from Silesia. AnnetteÂ´s and my ones lived surprisingly in the same area, less than 20 miles apart. We learned this only a couple of years ago.
Today this area belongs to South of Poland which is very close (< 10 miles) to Bohemian border.

C[thomas: Silesia is not a country, it is a region belonging to Poland, the Czech Rep. and also Germany. During WW2 it was German and at the end a lot of people were driven out and spread all over the world. Even Bohemia was German in WW2, better said it was violently annexed.
People from this area might have *mix mix* roots.
I heard that many US-citizens claim German ancestors. It might go back to the 19th century, but this expulsion in the 40s might be a 2nd wave.

C[Thomas: Dear or Hi? I am in a frequent contact with a high-ranked reseacher (Assoc. Prof.) in Philadelphia and a Senior Project Manager from the FDA in Washington (DC). We are all on first name basis. In every email I start with ""Dear ..."" and both always answer with ""Hi Thomas"". Is ""dear"" too direct, but ""hi"" appropriate? My feeling is there is a seismographic difference which a native knows, but a foreigner never will.

Misawa: Dear is very formal. I use it when talking to a loved one (as in my dearest...), or to be formal in which a Mr. or Ms. would be used (Dear Mr. Smith). Hi is more informal and casual. In normal communication, you can use Hi or just start with their name. Both are okay. So I would say - use Dear if it is a serious or formal letter, or a communication to a loved one. Use Hi, or just start with their name - no salutation at all - if it is ordinary communication about business or a project or whatever.

C[Thomas: That´s a really crucial point. In international business we turn very early to the first name basis. The unwritten rules are: We start with Dear Dr. X and sign the email with our full name. The US-responder resent with Dear Dr. Y and signs with his first name. We understand this as an polite offer and reply from now on with Dear John and something like Best, Thomas. Back to the example: I start very formal with Dear and the addressee replies with Hi. Does he/she invite me to be also casual and may be sort of disappointed when i respond again with the formal Dear? Do I decline unknowingly an unwritten rule or is it finally insignificant?

C[Misawa: Not really significant I think. Most people give someone who speaks English as a second language (i.e. non-native speakers) a lot of understanding and know better than to read too much into nuances of the language you use. Strange cultural nuances like this are frequently lost on non-native speakers so your specific words will not be scrutinized in this way. Generally, it is dangerous to try to read too much nuance in emails because it is so subjective. You are basically free to use what is comfortable to you.

C[Thomas: I like your view. Usually we are trained to identify every seismographic difference. That includes that we see ghosts where no ones are. From my perspective often native trainer from UK emphasize too often to be careful with these nuances. Eg. ""you may do"", ""you might do"" and ""you do"" are worlds apart. In Germany it wouldn´t run: the levels of training in their mother tongue are for all John Citizens too different. In short: Your recommendation to relax is refreshing.

C[Misawa: Yes, there are a lot of nuances like that. But I think most Americans realize that any non-native speaker can't be scrutinized for nuances and will take most language at face value with the kindest interpretation. British people can be much more stuffy and formal with their English. They criticize us Americans for messing up THEIR English language :-) They are probably right. But the good news is that most Americans are just relieved you speak enough English to be conversant and not force us to learn your language in order to communicate. We are too lazy for to learn other languages. Plus America is a melting pot with so many people from different places. We expect everyone to learn English - at least enough to get by. You can speak your own native language with your family and friends here and that is fine - but it better be some kind of passable english if you want to interact with others. Down here in the south - it is amazing how prevalent spanish is. It is everywhere. Most stores are bilingual. So the big exception to what I am saying is that in the southwest - you can often speak spanish to many people, though not officially. Where I am, there is a huge Iraqi (mostly Christian) population. So my sons have forms from his school come home in english, and spanish and arabic on the back!

C[Thomas: Very nice statement. You hit the nail on the hat. Nonetheless, I doubt that most Americans are just relieved we (foreigner) speak enough English to be conversant and do not force English natives to learn a foreign language in order to communicate. I my business life I often meet native English speakers who I have no idea and who take no pain to be just conversant or to use the language to bridge normal difficulties of their counterparts. I guess it is a lack of empathy. It appears they are not trained to use their mother tongue for its basic purpose: to understand other people. People who speak English as a foreign language are between each other far more careful in a conversation just to be conversant because they understand the distress of their counterparts. In Europe the revenge :-) is already under way. Non-native English speakers use more and more the Globish as lingua franca, its based on English but can only be spoken and understood by non-natives. But I have to concede that ""English"" is still the one and only European lingua franca. Especially in the scientific world. BUT: My swearing is not fair. Due to the fact that English is worldwide accepted as a communication tool, nearly all nations train this language, it is not esperanto, it is not Russian or Chinese (until now). Compared with that we should count our blessings every day :-)

C[Misawa: True, but I hope you find most Americans are patient and helpful. In the medical and engineering and scientific professions you often find people with some of the worst social skills. That may be more the issue than their ""American"" attitude. People in fields where they get lost in their work and have little interest in dealing with people are often a bit anti-social - I would add computer programmers and accountants to that as well. :-)
Misawa: And very true about Russian or Chinese - Lord help us all!

C[Thomas: Unfortunately, I cannot calculate 8 5'x8' boxes in to the German measures. A normal German furniture truck can load 35 cubic meters. I remember we needed appr. 1.5 trucks when we moved from Berlin. Each of your 8 boxes had *5'x8' x(height)* or *5'x8'x8'* ?
Thomas: Maybe too much figures, but it is interesting.
C[Misawa: it was eight: 5' x' 8' x 8' boxes. :-)

C[Thomas: If I am right it means in sum appr. 2.600 cu ft., which equals nearly one forty-foot equivalent unit in cargo transportation. These boxes can be transferred by ships, trains or trucks. I guess the truck was your choice?

C[Misawa: Each pod (basically a big plywood box), was about 300 CU feet or 8.5 CU Meters. So yes, about 2400 CU Ft or 68 CU M. We probably used about 80% of that space. Once we had the boxes loaded, the transport company came and picked them up. We flew out here the day after. Then after about a week and a half, the boxes arrived. Three days later, I had them emptied and they picked them up and took them away. All that cost a little under $5000. Not too bad. My car leases had expired, so I had no cars to move. column2: he cannot sell his expertise. In short: his assets are small. I can follow that the son or daughter as potential successor is a bliss. column1: C[Misawa: Right. I have known a few people who know they were going to inherit money. They were the most lost people I know. Finding a way to be happy being productive and useful somehow is critical to happiness - especially for men.

C[Thomas: Good point. Assuming you are forced to cut back either income or job satisfaction, which one is your lesser evil? If job satisfaction was defined by power and influence, would it hit men for six, even if the salary remains unchanged? Finally, I think we approach and validate our work with an economic mindset: we work because we are paid. Although we would always vow that our primary goal is to live and work with meaning and purpose. Even at the risk of talking inconsistently compared with my previous statements, I would choose the unchanged salary, because money is both a necessity and a major distraction.

C[Misawa: Well you have to balance it. You can love what you do, but if you aren't supporting yourself or your family - something has to be changed or supplemented. We all have to cover our obligations. But beyond that, then what? How do you determine what is satisfying? Is it a large pay check? Is it liking who  you work with? Is it feeling like what you do matters? Do you just love what you are doing for its own sake? All questions we have to balance. You spend an enormous amount of the time of your life at work and asleep. Better make both count!

(fast nur künftige Studenten oder Bankkaufleute) sollten sich zum Schulthema (!) G8 oder G9 äußern und die ""Jungs"" (in Überzahl) legten sich sofort mächtig ins Zeug
column2: für Julia ein komplett fremdes Thema ohne signifikante Beteiligungschance.

column1: Thomas: Mich erinnert das an deinen Satz ""das Niveau wird von Jahr zu Jahr schlechter"". Das System begünstigt „große Klappe“ mit Abitur, die nach drei Jahren wieder gehen, weil sie sowieso studieren wollten. Ich habe bei uns auch nichts mit Azubis zu tun, aber wir selektieren nach ähnlichen Kriterien und sind dann ärgerlich, wenn sich das Personal vorzeitig wieder verabschiedet.
C[RalfS: Die Hoffnung ist, dass sie nicht nur Menschen mit großer Klappe suchen. Ist nicht ganz unbegründet - stell Dir mal vor, in einer Filiale wären alle so agressiv wie diese Jungs

C[Thomas: Ich habe zumindest einige sympathische Menschen bei der Haspa kennengelernt und möglicherweise haben sich die ""Aggressiven"" sogar selbst rausgekickt, weil sie so nicht zum Unternehmen passen. In einer Runde von einem guten Dutzend wird der glückliche oder weniger glückliche Auftritt sicher nicht das allein ausschlaggebende Element sein. Am Ende gab es immerhin einen lehrreichen Einblick ist das rustikalere Hamburg.
Thomas: Julia sollte noch mal ihren (vollständigen) Lebenslauf mitbringen, was sie auch tat. Es ist der gleiche gewesen, den sie schon elektronisch eingereicht hat
column2: er fehlte also nicht. Sollten wir das klarstellen oder auf sich beruhen lassen?

C[Thomas: Heute schon kam die Absage. Ich glaube, es ist gut so
column2: es passte schlicht nicht zusammen. Nochmals Danke für deinen Einsatz.

GN[New Challenge for Thomas v Ken]
C[Misawa: Thanks for starting a new game!

Thomas: I have to admit that SD´s sum is not ""guilty"": Acc. to my records you won 9 of our last 10 games and my last win was in November 2013. It simply confirms your steady improvement. So, game on :-)

Thomas: I have to admit that SD´s sum is not ""guilty"": Acc. to my records you won 9 of our last 10 games and my last win was in November 2013. It simply confirms your steady improvement. So, game on :-)

C[Misawa: We have played 45 games? Is that just here, or are you counting at the other two servers we have played on? I wish it was steady improvement - very unsteady if you ask me. You are never give me an easy game.

C[Thomas: My records comprise all our games we have played at LG, DGS, even the single one at Dave Peck´s Go and here. The records cover all 14 years since our first move in Sept. 2003. If you like I´ll send an Excel file (a windows based excel 2013 file or if your Mac will cough as a PDF).
C[Misawa: Wow, that is amazing! I had no idea. 14 years! That is really something. One of these days we will need to actually meet!
Misawa: Please send me the file. Either excel or pdf. Whichever is easier for you.
C[Thomas: The email attachment is under way.

Thomas: The distance between us is ""only"" 5.7k miles. Despite of this figure, once we will meet for a board game face to face. Interestingly a fried of mine moved from TX to South Bend, IN, which is 2h away from Chicago. The pity is, my biz acitivities aren´t in both areas. In fact, our US business shrunk significantly (from my end). However, in September I will be back in Washington DC in order to defend our recent study results in a meeting with the competent authority.
C[Misawa: Too bad you don't have more business here - in California!
Misawa: I look forward to that face to face game

C[Thomas: In May I considered to attend the American Thoracic Society International Conference. No kidding, it took place in SD, but there was no chance for a second thought. Provided our regulatory efforts in the respiratory field will be finaly successful there is a good chance for 2015, but Denver it not California. And FDA refused already 3 times :(
C[Misawa: Ah so close! Well keep me posted. We'll get it figured out eventually
C[Misawa: That was a wicked move!
Misawa: Not sure I responded to that right
Thomas: It is. Some day I will even benefit from this move. Currently, I am not sure how :-)
C[Misawa: I would have dropped to B14. You then have a miai connection either way to your stone, removing the base for my stones
Misawa: Still your move is giving me fits
C[Thomas: B14 is now on the agenda, because of the ""fits""
C[Misawa: Just created a small private club (with a big name)
Misawa: Let's get some people to join and start a tournament
C[Thomas: Nice idea. And a fitting name :-)
C[Misawa: Not bad for one I made up on the spot  :-)
C[Misawa: Didi you sig up yet?

C[Thomas: There are moments I am anything than happy with thos server. I tried to sign up, but OGS replied ""This tournament is closed to provisional players. You need to establish your rank by playing ranked games before you can join this tournament"". Maybe, I can contact the admins. Last time it was tricky to get simply identified.

Thomas: I can play ladder games and I have no provisional rank. Insread in my games the rank vary from 5k to 13k and doe not change after ranked games. It is the old 11k story which was and is wrong from my perspective.

C[Misawa: I tried to edit the tournament to allow provisional - that didn't work. I tried to delete and recreate the tournament, that didn't work. I would check with the admins and ask them to remove the provisional status from your rank. You have enough games. It seems a mistake to me.
C[Thomas: Today I got the message: ""This account is currently marked as provisional until 5 more games have been played."" Just OGS :-(
Thomas: Good news is: I could easily join the ""Opening tournament"" without problems. :-)

C[Thomas: Obviously, it was my fault. There has been a mass rank adjustment recently. I adjusted my rank in my profile. This was the mistake. When you manually change your rank you are given 5 provisional games.
C[Misawa: I see. If you can get rid of the provisional status, I couldn't delete the other tournament, so people are joining it anyway...
C[Thomas: It will be close to Xmas, so patience is all :-)
C[Thomas: Tomorrow morning I will have take off to Washington DC. FDA is calling for a meeting.
C[Misawa: Have a safe trip. I think you need to tell your boss that the meeting got relocated to San Diego and you need to also fly over here!
C[Thomas: LOL, I agree, a video conference from SD should also work

C[Thomas: Yes, Washington was nice, I almost forgot to mention. You can say four days and 20 hours in the air are not well balanced with a 30 minutes meeting at the FDA. You can compare it with a sprinter, a certain period of 10 sec. can be very pivotal. In the regulatory business face to face contacts are decisive. We returned with unheard-of agreements. Besides, Capitol and the White House were musts again, the former even with a guided tour including the national pride in every sentence. Many likes from our side.
C[Misawa: D.C. is a really cool city. We were there a couple of years ago and really enjoyed it. I have to very close friends living there so we have an excuse to go back.

C[Thomas: In the Capitol several old oil paintings are shown, depicting milestones in the US history. I missed the ""Washington Crossing the Delaware"" from I thought it is the most significant in the US history. Later I read there is a copy in the White House, not in the Capitol. The (latest) original is in the MET in NYC. Next time.
C[Misawa: Ah, the amount of art that is in D.C. is amazing. Did you get to do any Smithsonian museums?

C[Thomas: Our stopover in the city was short, we had to return to the hotel in order to write a further email to FDA´s project manager. So, Smithsonian museums could not be on the list, but if we had had more time, we would have been too tired for a longer excursion.
Thomas: Unfortunately, I have no precise records how often I was in the states. I guess NYC and DC is in lead, but I never planned sufficient time for a touristic program.
Misawa: Well in all your traveling, you should do the States some time. There's so much to see!

C[Thomas: That´s right. LA, SF, NYC, DC. Atlanta, Miami, Phoenix, Las Vegas are already on my list and I touched ground of 10 States. Today I read in the INYT about Tulare Country, which I may have crossed in the 80s: the state´s record-breaking drought with vanishing lakes and rivers and now even the disappearance of tap water. Do you also suffer from the very long lasting (first) summer in SD?

C[Misawa: It has rained here about twice since February. Both time about 1/8 inch or so. So basically nothing. Water is very expensive. We still have the water we need to irrigate the orchard. But we pulled out the grass in our yard and replaced it with rocks and succulents. I will and pix.
C[Thomas: Was this high price for water expected or a surprise? Maybe, it is a comfort: for 1,000 liters tap water we have to pay 6.75 USD
C[Misawa: Our water bill for two months of water was $400! We knew it would be higher, but honestly we are convinced the previous owner misrepresented her utilities.

C[Thomas: I checked our records, we paid 1,700 USD/year for the tap water (it includes the water for the garden) when we were four. So, 400 bucks appear very much. My guess is that the maintenance costs of a pool eat lots of money, because so much evaporate.

Misawa: Yeah, that is about what we would pay in Kentucky. The pool is a factor, but the orchard is a much bigger factor. The biggest factor though is the drought. It has not rained in months. We are going to have an el nino this winter, so hopefully that will bring rain. The plus side of this is we just harvested our pomegranates and the avocados and oranges are on their way.
C[Misawa: Up for tournament 3? One spot left!

Thomas: Unbelievable, but our pomegranate has two blossoms and the lemon tree one fruit. The harvest isn´t that big, but we are happy that even Mediterranean plants sometimes blossom here. No comparison to your new experience. As a boy I dreamt of a garden full of succulents and cacti. This dream was limited to indoor plants - in past. Or it comes back when we are in Italy as it happened in the previous week. In this way I´m jealous about your plus side.
Thomas: Tournament 3? I am far behind the average player in tournament 1. But even so under pressure to adhere to the clock. Being honest, at about ten games at the same time is all I can cope with.
C[Misawa: My wife is obsessed with succulent plants. She might have some pictures of them on her Facebook page, I can't remember
C[Thomas: I saw it on FB and appreciate it. In this way SD must be her paradise. Do you expect a crazy halloween in SD as well as you know from KY?
C[Misawa: Sadly no. We are missing our old Halloween street very much. Nothing close to  hereit

C[Thomas: Although I have no chromosomes for it, I know it was your high light in October. My guess, it happened because Amethel´s b-day should find itself more in the spotlight. Convey many congratulations.
C[Misawa: True and I will. Today is her day. My younger son's class went to the zoo today, so she went along. A nice reprieve from work and good preparation for some fun tonight.

C[Misawa: Traveling back to Louisville on Saturday for 9 days. It is cold there. We are in a panic because the kid's winter clothes are too small from last year and so we have to buy cold weather clothes for them.
C[Thomas: It´s a joke, but: A 2,000 miles flight for cold weather clothes? Do you think you will need them in SD?
Thomas: Have a nice and safe flight :-)
C[Thomas: Well, I missed togay´s T-Day and Black Friday for cold weather clothes. That makes sense. Enjoy your family around and later the famous pumpkin pie.
C[Misawa: Done and done! Pie - eaten! Cold weather - check! Family - enjoyed! It's been a great trip. I am missing CA. We head back on Tuesday. How have you been?

C[Thomas: Great news. Successful recovery till Tuesday. You know, we have no equivalent for your national state of emergency on T-Day. We have enjoyed an almost relaxing time these days, mostly inside, because temps turned down to 30F and a strong wind from the East entertains us.

C[Misawa: Nice. It was 30 the other day with snow flurries - and today over 60. Weird. We've been cleaning the basement of our KY house (we left a lot of stuff locked up down there). Had a yearly staff dinner with my people. Seeing lots of family and friends. But we are starting to miss our home. And our weather.
C[Misawa: We are home and we had rain! Almost 3"" which is a good start
Thomas: 3"" and all in one? Must be the normal rate of a whole year. At least some evidence that rain is still possible.
C[Misawa: Yeah, it was great. Turned the irrigation off for a week or so. We should get an el nino this year. So hopefully that will relieve the drought a bit

C[Thomas: Some German well-offs enjoy winter time in warm areas, e.g.  in Spain like Majorca. But they often mourn that Xmas isn´t the same anymore. Warm temps, sunshine, beach and Xmas trees don´t match. Is this your latest experience, too?

C[Misawa: I think I am open to a new experience so I liked what we did. I was ready to create our own experience and traditions. So I was happy this year. I am still not tired of the sunny weather :-)
Misawa: Merry Christmas to you and your family by the way! I hope it was a good time for you.
C[Thomas: Your answer confuses me a little bit. Did my humorously comparison give any offense?

Misawa: No not at all! The notion of a warm and sunny Christmas has been a hot topic of discussion. We keep asking - is this weird and do we miss our cold KY Christmas. Personally, I don't miss it. I am happy with a new kind of Christmas. People here go crazy with Christmas lights and decorations - because the weather does not match what people imagine Christmas should look like. I think it is very fun. I am enjoying the change. I do NOT mourn the cold! :-)
C[Thomas: We have a dilemma. Personally, I also do not mourn the cold. But it is still not really cold and the lack is poison for our turn-over.
C[Misawa: I don't understand: ""poison for our turn-over""

C[Thomas: Our company´s turn-over is jeopardized by mild winters. In this sense the last season was close to a disaster.  A second one must not occur. Of course, only ""others"" have to suffer, although I started yesterday evening with a drug-demanding cold.

C[Misawa: You lose people when you had a mild winter? I would have thought the opposite. People are fleeing Chicago because they can't stand another deathly cold winter. - Feel better. Having a cold is no fun.
C[Thomas: In a mild winter people don´t have colds or at least at a lower rate. They will buy cold medications less or not at all. We (the company) run a risk to get strapped for cash.
C[Thomas: It remains an ethical dilemma.
C[Misawa: Yeah, I could see that. Good thing there is ""No cure for the common cold!""

C[Thomas: LOL, however the climate change tries to eradicate the cold. Finally, our cold medication was successful again. We try to launch this medication also in the US, in order to balance this injustice. Only, FDA is still averse to doing this.

C[Misawa: Why are they averse? It amazes me that there is not an easier route for reciprocity between the U.S. and Europe on things like medicines. But then, the same is true for providing architectural services. License reciprocity between Europe and U.S. for architecture has been talked about forever, but still not an easy task.

Thomas: Cough & cold medications in the US are somewhat obscure. Most often only known from traditional use and their regulatory pathways not approved by FDA. The prices are low. A real drug approval demands to conduct all clinical trials again, this time only with US citizens from coast to coast. In short: a 10m investment. Shortcuts were possible for some competitors in past, but do not work anymore. One spot: we have to submit animal trials who tolerated at least a 100 times higher dosage than humans would normally use. No comment.

Thomas: License reciprocity is a probelm also for me. If I moved to the US, I would be a nothing. No chance to work as a physician. A job in the pharmaceutical industry would be possible, however the salary would be cut in half.
C[Misawa: Very true. Many of the filipino doctors and nurses who emigrate here face this. It is a tough road to get certified here.
C[Misawa: I totally missed your comment about [11:44\]. Wow that is nuts. $10M is an intense investment. So is your drug very different than our cold medicines?
C[Thomas: Of course, I have an optimism bias, when I confirm a YES. Most of your cough & colds don´t have a FDA approval
column2: they are dietary supplements or homeopathics. FDA is ready to tidy them up. Most of our drugs which facilitate expectoration have a traditional or well-established use
column3: nowadays they have to be supported by modern studies. FDA approval also ask for clinical trials, but most often they will be excluded from any reimbursement, so it is not attractive.

column1: C[Misawa: Huh. I had no idea. And I will say that probably most Americans don't know that either. Can't you see your product in similar terms

C[Thomas: That is an issue that consumer do not notice and companies print that only in mousetype. We try hard, but we cannot (and will not) submit an long-term toxicity study in rats or dogs just to demonstrate what we already know in far higher dosages. And we only want to see our product in ""similar terms"". Nothing else.

C[Misawa: So I am reading a very interesting Go book. Do you have the App ""Go Books"" on your iPad? I highly recommend it if you don't. There is a series of books there called ""Master Play"" and the pick various pros and explain their style of play. The commentator is very good at making the games understandable. They are also short and inexpensive. I am reading the one about Go Seigen. I'm enjoying it a lot. It is helpful to understand what the next levels of play are about so that you can at least see the outlines of the map forward. At least know the names of the destinations :-)

Thomas: That is really interesting. I saw it in the store, but hesitated. The list of books titles I can see consists of ten, added by 16 front covers. The Master Play of Go Seigen is not included, but three other ones. I checked it: The German version appears to be the same as the US one. I guess I´ll take it :)
C[Thomas: Got it. Although it competes with Grisham´s classical tragedy ""a time to kill""

Misawa: Just finished the Go Seigen one. Now I want to get more of them. I always hear you should study other professional games, but without clear commentary, it is like listening to a foreign language. I need a very good translator! But once you start to learn the newer language, it does start to become more understandable. Now I begin to see the wisdom. It makes you look at your own game more critically.
Misawa: Ah Grisham is tough competition indeed!

C[Thomas: It is amazing to read in a ""book"" and replay short sequences again and again. The comments are a little bit funny (""searching for the truth""), but I entertain them. German go game comments are often directed by a Korean 8p and it is too often stressed on big mistakes pros ""allowed"" themselves. For me it is not helpful. As you emphasized this book is different and I stopped reading Grisham.

C[Misawa: Right! I like that Go Seigen will just determine for himself where the game is and ignore sent moves - often at huge cost. I often ignore sent moves - at great cost - but only because I missed their significance! :-)

C[Thomas: Sente is a different flight level. I don´t know when it is on or off. It will come later in life :-)    In school my major subjects were maths and arts. The focus of the latter was art history. We analysed all famous canvasses and found clear evidences why these genuis´ painted their masterpieces exactely like they were. A magic hand lead the brushes and all rules yield to the one and only.  Spectators hang on their lips. Finally a pure marvel. I always thought they just paint, sometimes natural, sometimes experimental and then they wait what crazy people will interpret. Many zeros make the ""one"" big. Sometimes I recollect this old perception. Did Go Seigen really plan every move resp. a sequence of them or did he just try and wait. Often he was just lucky, if not, he allowed his opponent ceremonially a benefit.

C[Misawa: I suspect his insane ability to win is a clue that it was more than just luck. He could anticipate the direction of play in a way that was deeply intuitive. Maybe able to imagine a lot of long sequences and pick the most likely and be ready.
Misawa: Sente is tough. It only works if the opponent agrees that this move is the most dangerous on the board. Seigen would just not agree where most would. That is what is so nutty about his games.

C[Thomas: Of course Go Seigen is currently in our headlines. And many emphasize that he is the best Go player ever. I don´t want to put this in question, at least I cannot assess it at all. But why is the admiration so great and when started it. In the last 50 years he was regarded as someone whose stamina and concentration has greatly deteriorated due to an accident. He retired virtually 51 years ago and he brought only very few title or tournament victories at home. An interesting vita.
Thomas: Nonetheless, I fully agree that it is of value to replay and try to understand the comments of games of high flight players. Maybe, I am also an admirer soon :-)

Misawa: He died a few months ago - at the age of 100! He also was responsible for some big changes in go theory (fuseki). I am actually only recently an admirer. I am a big fan of Xie Yimin. But I can't say I know very much about her style of play.
C[Thomas: Xie Yimin - never heard. Be sure, I will dig :-)

C[Thomas: Happy New Year combined with the same wishes. Taking your recent improvement into account, more go might the key for me. Especially in December I learned (again) it is beyond my capacities. I am often astonished how much time you (and others) can spend for playing go. Maybe it is the own office which allows access when ever you want. For me OGS isn´t open before 6 or 7 PM. C´est la vie.

C[Misawa: I think the key is playing more games at once. If you play once a day, and only make a few moves, it is harder to really get in the mindset. For me - the biggest change is just trying harder to not be lazy when I play. To really look for good move, not fall victim to wishful thinking, not just follow the other player around, and to realize that a sequence is going to play out badly for me before I start it. These a big ones for me.

C[Thomas: Both of your tenets are consistent with what Yuan Zhou recommended in „How not to play go“. As basics I agree. From my perspective they work regularily, when you play against a weaker opponent and will (always) fail, when the other player is stronger. For all other games „smile, breathe and go slowly“ is the head note. Playing more games is often advised, but demands time. If you invested this (bunch of) time, you might be rewarded with something pleasurable (e.g. more wins), that´s the addictive part of go. If not, you have to cope with some frustration (too much defeats against suspected stronger opponent). I learned that playing more games divided my attention even further and impeded to get into the mindset. And one have to balance the addictive quality of instant positive feedback and delayed negative feedback, a yes to more games demands several „no“ to other activities (e.g. sport). Currently, I only make a few moves several times a week. Being honest, the price of go defeats is sometimes high. I would lie to claim that I am happy with my current go performance. But as ever, you can´t have it all. Any strategy? Yes, playing predominantly in tournaments with a higher theoretical chance to win and avoiding to contrary. Confining to games with an appropriate time buffer. In addition, alternating focus and rest. Happy to see, when friends establish a different approach and are successful.

C[Misawa: It is true that it demands more time - and your citation of ""How not to play go"" is right on. I read it and was embarrassed to see myself there. I had a Korean guy in a go club yell at me for playing sloppy - that kick in the pants was also embarrassing but transforming. I also had a young girl (about 15 or so years younger than me - she was a kid at the time) ask me - don't you see the patterns? Haven't you learned them yet?"" And I have been trying to work out what she was talking about ever since (this was years ago). Because she beat me every time - badly. So, trying to just realize the obvious outcome of a move and evaluate it before I make it - is it what I really want? What will happen? What direction do I want to play? What shape am I hoping for? Where are my eyes? Can I still connect to those nearby stones? What can I cut that will be useful? Where can pressure be applied? Where can I push? These are the questions I try to slow down and ask myself. I need to play a lot of games to test and learn ""the patterns"" as my young friend suggested.

C[Thomas: It is a nice story about your inner drive and I can only recommend to keep this passion as long as it fits into life. I have to admit that my job forces me to rush and I try to make make changes in what I do and in how I (will) work (or my staff should do). In order to migitate this daily engagement in a „harrying blur of multitasking activity“ (not my invention), I have rediscovered the beauty of disconnection. I am not saying I must get out (of OGS), and I often struggle with it myself, but the key is simple: try to focus. Probably that is really not your subject. But, let me pose a rhetorical question: How many time are you distracted or tempted to switch to OGS during your „normal“ office hours? I guess it´s not „zero“.

C[Misawa: Less than I used to actually. I try to make the time more quality play time, rather than playing a lot of short quick sessions. That is also one thing I changed. I play less frequently. Sometimes if I am doing a house task, I might leave it open during the evenings and play someone who shows up in a more ""live"" mode. But that is rare. I used to use go as a mental palette cleanser between tasks. That lead to very sloppy play - and more distraction. So I have stopped doing that. Do I stop in more than I should - of course! It is GO after all. Hard to stay away. I am actually playing less, which is making my play better. More games. Fewer daily sessions. Strange, but it is working.
C[Thomas: I see, I it/was your subject, too. Sounds healthy.

This confuses the old teams. Perfect pattern for me to use all available management tools. The real disadvantage are long office hours to balance this ""temporary"" dysfunction.

C[Misawa: So I had a win I was particularly proud of:https://online-go.com/game/1373234
Misawa: I was losing up until move 243. I saw that monkey jump and knew it was a mistake. You can't monkey jump when the stone you are jumping from (M18) has a weak connection to its base.
Misawa: We were playing live at this point - so when i played N18, he did not see the danger.
Misawa: So to be even meaner after move 272, I killed the rest of the his upper right corner.

Misawa: To add further insult to injury, I noticed that his bottom side had a big problem too. Move 280 started another sequence that I am particularly proud of. Usually I am on the receiving end of this kind of attack. Rarely do I get to pull something like this off.
Misawa: 10 moves later his bottom side was destroyed as well and that was the game. All of this last stuff was played live over about an hour.
Misawa: Hollasch is a tough player, so I was happy to pull this loss into a victory.

Thomas: Wow ! I replayed your game several times on good old CGoBan3 which still runs on the laptop. If he had played M17 instead of M19 and sacrified the little group at the top the end would have been tight. Your Q15 was a godsend, the corner was turned. Many congrats for this masterstroke.

C[Misawa: Exactly. The problem with the monkey jump is that you have to make the last defensive move and give sente to your opponent. When you have a weak connection, you can attack that connection. Missing M17 was the first problem.
C[Thomas: This large monkey jump at J19 was too greedy and the weak M18 best precondition for a failure. i am not sure whether my reading would have been good enough to predict black´s disaster.
C[Misawa: I saw the beginning of the problem, but even I was surprised once it started that it went that far.

C[Misawa: We listed at $495,000. The buyer approached me before the house was even on the market. He wanted to me to keep it off the market and deal direct. We said no. C[Thomas: A banker told me that in our area most often the ownership for an ""old house"" changes for €150k or less. But it is usually put on the market for something like €180k. C[Thomas: Jetzt klingelt es: das Tychonische Weltmodell. Wir hatten im Frühjahr eine Veranstaltung, in der es um die Rationalität der Wissenschaft gehen sollte. Das Thema fand leider keine Mehrheit, aber Brahe begegnete mir dabei am Rande. Der Inselname hätte nichts bewirkt, aber der ungewöhnliche Vorname ließ mich in meinen Vorbereitungen blättern.

Sehr aufschlussreich, wie die beiden Zeitgenossen miteinander umgingen,und Kepler war ja der Nutzer der Daten der Braheschen Beobachtungen.

C[Thomas: Sorry, lieber Ralf, die Einladung hatte ich übersehen. Ein schönes Spiel für dich.
RalfS: Alles ok :) Lieber Thomas, möge es spannend und schön auch für Dich werden!
C[Thomas: Es ist ziemlich windig hier - oder warum wackelt diese Gruppe so ...

C[Thomas: Lieber Ralf, sorry für das Aufgeben des anderen Spiels. Ich werde dieses noch zu Ende spielen und dann in einen Spielpause übergehen. Momentan schaffe ich es schlicht zeitlich nicht, Go zu spielen.

C[RalfS: Lieber Thomas, sehr merkwürdig, dass dieses zeitgleich bei Dir und auch mir auftritt. Ich habe mich vor drei Tagen entschlossen, aus dem DGoB auszutreten, mein Bundesliga-Engagement niederlegen und auch die Spiele auf den Servern reduzieren. Allerdings will ich noch so 5 Spiele aufrecht erhalten. Es liegt bei mir wohl am Stress der letzten Berufsjahre.

C[Thomas: Das mit dem DGoB teile ich ebenfalls und bin vor ein paar Wochen zur Tat geschritten. Einerseits habe ich ein paar Bälle zu viel in der Luft, aber es haben sich auch meine Freizeitaktivitäten etwas verlagert, so dass Go spielen automatisch knapper bei wegkommt. Darunter litt auch die Spielfreude, so dass eine Pause auch deswegen naheliegt.
C[RalfS: Dann freue ich mich, dass wir noch ein Spiel aufrechterhalten! :)
RalfS: Lieber Thomas, herzlichen Glückwunsch und vor allem Gesundheit und viel Freude am Leben!
C[Thomas: Lieber Ralf, über euren Kartengruß haben wir uns sehr gefreut. Herzlichen Dank an Ingrid und dich. Wir wünschen euch ebenfalls frohe Festtage und einen guten Rutsch ins neue Jahr.
C[RalfS: Danke :) Diesmal habe ich schon ab 13.12. Urlaub - so war es denn wesentlich entspannter, alles um Weihnachten herum zu erledigen. ... Die Eckgruppe ist nun gerettet, der Preis ist hoch
C[Thomas: Das mit der Eckgruppe war schon gut gemacht.
C[Thomas: Ein frohes neues Jahr für euch, lieber Ralf.
C[RalfS: Lieber Thomas, danke - das wünschen wir euch ebenso. Wir haben diesmal zu zweit zuhause gut gegessen, von innen Feuerwerk geschaut und s
RalfS: ... und später entspannt den Morgen begrüßt. Wart ihr ausser Haus?

C[Thomas: Bei uns war der Ablauf der gleiche wie bei euch. Wir haben zwar mal darüber nachgedacht, irgendwo etwas ferner in einem Hotel den Jahreswechsel zu erleben, aber die Kosten und der zu erwartete Krach hat uns immer davon abgehalten. Ehrlich gesagt, ginge es nach uns, bräuchte Silvester nicht statt zu finden
column2: ich war schon früher kein Fan von Reinfeiern, weil sich das auch in netter Runde hinziehen kann.

GN[Tournament Game: Tournament 14 (13935) R:3 (Thomas vs ruster)]
C[ruster: hello, have a good game!
C[ruster: hello, have a good game!
Misawa: Hi Thomas, thanks for playing me again.
C[Thomas: I wish you and your family a merry Christmas, too.
C[Thomas: I wish you all a happy New Year
C[Misawa: You too! I hope you enjoy good health and fulfilling work! Please give our best to your family.
C[Thomas: Many thanks for the wishes. We work on both topics :-)
C[Misawa: Do you do Netflix? If so, check out the Alphago movie. It's really good. Good story telling. Interesting from both a go perspective and a technology perspective too.

Thomas: Thanks for the hint. We do not Netflix. It is here not so popular. We still watch TV via satellite. We have a quite good internet in our area, but not for streaming movies. Next month we will have fiber optic cables, promising a far better internet speed, but there is no reason to update to Netflix, amazon, sky, maxdome or what ever. We enjoy around 100, even 20 good digital TV programs in HD plus quality for less than 6 Euros per month. For the Alphago movie it is a pity.
C[Misawa: Wow. You may be able to stream it for fairly cheap though. Possibly find it on You Tube. My wife even enjoyed it.
C[Misawa: Finally making plans to return to Europe - and Africa!  We’ll be in southern Spain and Morocco at the end of May and first of June. Our
Thomas: Sounds like Marrakesh and Alhambra. Perfect places for a quarter century. :-)
C[Thomas: In May we will have vacations too. but we are a little late for booking. Still no decision, but a safe area, not too far away.
Misawa: Awesome. We are still arranging the itenary. So far it looks like Mallorca, Fez, then Southern Spain road trip back to Barcelona.
C[Thomas: I have never been in Morocco, Fez sounds like 1001 nights.

Thomas: Mallorca: That´s hardcore tourism. It is said the island is already part of Germany. Don´t expect to have a sleep. My daughters enjoyed the area very much. They only said the drunken Germans were embarrassing.
C[Misawa: We'll be sending some time on Minorca too - the island to the east of Mallorca. But thanks for the warning. We'll keep that in mind as we book our hotels.

C[Thomas: The capital of Mallorca, especially the area called ""Ballermann"" gave the name ""party island"", the proper name of a drinking establishment there. A mekka for young people. Nonetheless, the Balearic Islands are very nice.
C[Misawa: We're getting everything in order. So excited. Been too long since we've been abroad.
C[Thomas: And this time it is from CA and not KY. It will last to get abroad :-)
C[Misawa: Yes - much farther. I think our flight from LA to Barcelona is 11 hours
C[Thomas: 11h - That's sheer madness, but inevitable. Afterwards you will need physiotherapy.

Thomas: For my (young) colleagues a trip to Australia or New Zealand seems to be a must. And they take pain for a 24 to 30h flight and claim afterwards only there you can really relax at a maximum. I often think there must be a translation error for ""relaxing"".
C[Misawa: Are they talking about the flight? Relaxing? I’m with you, that sounds like torture.
C[Thomas: I guess you bought four tickets for your vacations?
C[Misawa: Just two! My parents are coming out to stay with the kids. They get a vacation too
C[Misawa: Let's hope! I hope they enjoy being here for almost a month with the kids. It will be a first.
C[Thomas: A full month is a big deal, but some day it is always the first time. Knowing that the own parents take care helps.
C[Thomas: Finally, it is an experiment for both sides. Also parents may have a home sickness :-)

Misawa: Very true on all accounts. My older son has become a very good photographer. I know we’ll be thinking about how much he would have enjoyed being with us. My younger son is just so funny and crazy that i’ll Just miss laughing at him.
C[Thomas: A reliable care at home is mandatory, but it is difficult to clear the mind far away.  Later in life this feeling does not subside, but a geographical distance becomes normal.
C[Misawa: Yeah, that’s coming soon enough. I dread it.
C[Thomas: It will happen that your older son plans the same trip to Europe (+ Fez) as you do now - but without you. And your heart is inclined to say ""no"".
C[Misawa: I think you are right! I'm having a hard time with the idea he might be gone for a month this summer
C[Misawa: First college visit is this weekend...UGH!
C[Thomas: Just a visit to check or first day up to the bachelor´s degree?
C[Misawa: Visit to check. He still has another school year. But this is the beginning of the process.

C[Thomas: It happens very early in the US. Here academic degrees start with the bachelor (3 yrs.) and maybe continued by an master program (2 yrs.). (Only) the latter qualifies for a (research) doctoral degree. M.D. are professional degrees (6 yrs.).

Thomas: In my job interviews I most often meet applicants with a M.Sc. oder Ph.D. who have no idea what they want to do for a living. They start to think about it when they are already in the 30s - we talk about fresh graduates.
Thomas: Your son has to answer the difficult question in which he will sometimes work. So early in life it is a challenge, especially one ""yes"" results in several ""no"". Does he have an idea?
C[Misawa: He says he wants to be an architect. I think he could do it. I could also see him being an engineer (structural). The summer program will really let him know I think.
C[Thomas: Summer program? Does the highschool offer something like a taster course?

C[Misawa: It's at the university. It's like a taste of college for 4 weeks, but probably more fun. They live on campus, got to architecture classes, go on some tours, and have to produce projects. I did it when I was between my Junior and Senior year. It was awesome.

C[Thomas: It appears that the 4 wks. taste is focussed on architecture.  What happens afterwards when e.g. architecture is only the second option and one will check an alternative e.g. engineer. Is there a second chance to taste?
C[Misawa: No. Thus it’s a big decision, and important opportunity

C[Thomas: I see, you may taste only after the decision. But you are happy about at least this opportunity. Here there is no chance to taste, you may apply and wait. In bigger cities in vain for sure. Instead, private universities sprang up like mushrooms - they are expensive, but compared with the US we have been blessed in this matter so far.
Thomas: It is said that US universities are expensive. Is it possible for a young student to afford a life in far-out cities or do they life then still at home?

C[Misawa: You are right, university educations here are very expensive. The hope is for scholarships and grants to help. Kai has had all A's his entire school career. So fingers crossed. We visited a school called Cal Poly (California Polytechnic) an engineer and agriculture focused university. It is a state school (not a private university). So he can get in-state tuition which is much less than if he went to a private university or one in a different state.

Misawa: So the good news, it is the second rated undergraduate program in the US for architecture and is only about $10k a year. The bad news, the other fees and housing put that cost at $30k a year and he has to go or 5 years. Ugh. So hopefully scholarships and other savings emerge. Getting accepted is very competitive. I think he can do it. But we are bracing for some horrific costs.
Misawa: For perspective - the top school for architecture is Cornell in New York. It would cost over $70k a year for him to go there - plus be 3000 miles from us. Non-start.
Misawa: Cal Poly is only 300 miles away. And doesn't have winter.
Thomas: Is it common that parents pay the full sum or is it kind of loan that the student will pack back when he/she is in the job?
Thomas: 5 yrs. for an undergraduate program? Will it end with a bachelor? Here bachelor and master last five years and in science the bachelor is often regarded as the small academic.
Thomas: Is it necessary to study at high ranked universities? Here the grades decide if you get an invitation for a job interview and what soft skills you offer. The university is ancillary at best.

C[Misawa: Scholarships, grants, and loans hopefully reduce the price. Also, after freshman year he can move off campus and stop paying for on-campus housing (that part alone was over $13k). Yes, architecture is unusual in that it is 5 years, not 4. Some are even 6, but they lead to a masters degree.

Misawa: Is it necessary to study at a high ranked university - no, but it is helpful. The quality of education is and opportunities after college are better. Even if he ultimately works for me, I hope he comes in and shakes things up with all that he has learned.

C[Thomas: The fees and other costs are no pushover. Well-bings may afford this. What happens to lets say architects from lower ranked universities. Do they hope to be successor of a parent´s office or do the y
Thomas: ... or do they become a taxi-driver?
C[Misawa: LOL, no they should get work. But maybe it is hard to find a good job.
Misawa: Or they are not as well trained or skilled.

C[Thomas: I´d guess all universities in the US offer the same standardized program. Seemling a B-grade at an A-university is better than an A-grade in a B-university, the latter is regarded as not so well trained.
Thomas: Any grade at an A-university is expensive and not designed for everybody. Sorry, my thoughts are too German-driven :-)

Thomas: Here the average grades of all subjects rule the access to the university. Independent from the school (""college"") your average school grades have to meet a numerus clausus. In medicine it is currently a mean 1.0 of all grades to start at a German university in medicine. I don´t you, if this figure can be translated into consistent A grades in your system. There are schools where it is said that the high mean grade is easier to achieve than in other schools. You have no choice. You will go to the college in your neighbourhood. But significant changes are already planned. Now you have to pass also a test which consists of questions you are intended to learn later in the university. First the bachelor than the access to the university. Crackbrained.

C[Misawa: Interesting. There is a standardized test here (two actually) that have a lot to do with access to universities and scholarships. It's a very hard test and high scores are mandatory. Good grades are not enough without these test scores.
C[Misawa: Up for another game? I'm beating a dead horse here. Well played - congrats!
C[Thomas: Yes, it is bold. Have a nice game.
C[Misawa: Amethel leaves for Minorca in one week. I follow her one week later!
Misawa: I’m headed to Minorca on Sunday! Can’t wait!
Thomas: I guess Minorca is 1,000 miles away from here. Currently in many parts of Europe we have the blackberry winter after real summer days. Hopefully Minorca is far away from us.

C[Misawa: Just got here last night. Took almost 24 hours of travel! But it is beautiful. Just had a nice European breakfast. Amethel has already been here for a week. She’s going to show me around for a couple of days. Then off to Barcelona
C[Thomas: Enjoy. It is said Menorca is famous for its cheese, provided you like products from cow´s milk.
Thomas: Maybe you are already more familiar with the Iberian culture than me.
C[Misawa: We have had some very nice cheese and sausage. Very peaceful here. Love it! We'll be putting some pictures up on Facebook.
C[Misawa: Arrived in Barcelona this morning. Quick stop. Leave the day after tomorrow for Morocco.
C[Misawa: Valencia tonight. Barcelona tomorrow. Then San Diego. Almost time to be home.
C[Thomas: Sounds like rush hour. I am happy that you both returned from the medieval trip :-)

C[Misawa: Rolling into San Diego now. Our flight took us to LA. Hired a Tesla to drive us back to San Diego. I’m writing you from that car now. Home in about 30 minutes! It’s about 1 AM here. Not sure what time my body thinks it is. Awesome trip!

Thomas: Perfect, you made it. I never experienced a restful slumber during a long distance flight. It took me at least the next night to get all chromosomes in line again. Get well soon and thx for sharing this awesome trip.
C[Misawa: Thank you. Yesterday was a weird jet lag day. Today...I just don't want to get back to work - but I'm trying. It was an awesome trip indeed. Still many photos to process.
C[Misawa: Looks like this one is done. Up for another?
C[Thomas: Many thanks for the game. I´m up for another :-)
Thomas: For the next game I again offer that you take black. Hopefully the invitation addresses this adequately. If not, decline.
Misawa: Looks good. Thank you for this game. See you on the next board!
C[Thomas: Welcome back. Have a nice game.
C[Thomas: Happy Easter for you and your family!
C[Misawa: Thank you and same to yours! Do you have plans with your daughters?
Thomas: Interestingly, they were both busy. So, enough time for short trips in twos.
C[Misawa: How old are they now? I’ve lost track.
C[Thomas: This year they we will turn 26 and 24 yrs. The sum may ring :-)
Misawa: Wow! That's amazing. How long have we been playing go together? It's been a long time!
C[Thomas: It is really amazing. You were 34 yrs., when we started our first game on 09-Sep-2003 on ""Little Golem"". In autumn 16 Years !

C[Misawa: Wow! That's nuts. So almost 16 years. I haven't been to Little Golem in years. I forgot that we started playing there. How funny. Can you still sign in and see how many games we played there?
C[Thomas: I have only my own records (excel file): 12 on LG, 15 on DGS (1 on Dave Peck's Go). 2007 we moved to OGS.
C[Misawa: 56 games! (If my math is right). Wow!

C[Thomas: 56 games is correct. I guess we share the joy in stats: Today I send our 11.295th move. That can be translated in 3.3 moves per day on average, every day in close to 16 years. That´s my wow! :-)
C[Misawa: Wow! That's wild! The things this game makes you do!
Misawa: By the way, have you seen the movies ""The Surrounding Game"" and ""AlphaGo""?
Misawa: Still can't get over it - 11,296 moves!
C[Thomas: Assumed, one move has taken 5 sec., all moves have been made in some more than 15 hours. That sounds like nothing in 16 years. In fact, I consider some moves over days back and forth.
C[Thomas: I haven´t seen both movies. Saw a trailer of the first one.
Misawa: Great stars. Just amazing. The guys who play live simultaneous games play almost that fast.
Misawa: Definitely check the movies out. Both are very good.
C[Thomas: Not sure what I actually planned with O1. Must have been the temps around 90F.
C[Misawa: LOL, that will do it. I try to play when I’m tired. Not a good idea.
C[Misawa: Sounds like you guys have had quite the heat wave?

C[Thomas: Wave is correct. 90F come and go.  This week we enjoy only 60F. The main problem is that it does not rain. In past a steady rainy weather, now nearly nothing. Still not a drought as last year, but it is still early.

C[Misawa: That's hot for your area. We aren't supposed to get much warmer than 85-90 degrees. Last year we had a whole week above 95. Maybe longer. It reached 112. Nasty nasty nasty. That was in June or July. It didn't rain until October or so. We've had light rain all the way into June this year. It will likely not rain again until October. It's very weird to go so long without rain.

C[Misawa: We just got home from a small tour of the southern and central California. We went to LA, Morro Bay, Sequoia National Park, and Yosemite National Park. It was a LOT of driving. California is just huge. But it was so beautiful. Keep an eye on our Facebook pages. Images to come!
C[Thomas: Many thanks for this invitation. When Germans think of their first trip to the US, the South-West is the answer.
C[Misawa: I can see why. It's another world.
C[Thomas: It was the highway 1 from SF down to LA, via Monterey, Big Sur, later on 101. California dreamin from Beach Boys. Mooro Bay must be famous for locals or I missed a hot spot :-)
Thomas: In fact, when Germans think of the South West , they long for the Monument Valley in Utah or Grand Canyon and the Sierra Nevada is something to pass.
C[Misawa: Interesting. We have really enjoyed being in this amazing state. We continue to be amazed by it. Utah is definitely on the list.

C[Thomas: In the 80s I travelled with a friend from Death Valley to SF by car, but I can´t recall the route. It was not Sequoia or Yosemite area and we did not pass Huntington Lake which you might have visited. Maybe, somewhere south of Reno. These amazing landscapes form your memories for the whole life.
Misawa: Very true. We feel so lucky that these places are within easy (easier) reach. Do you have any travel plans coming up? Amethel leaves for the Faroe Islands within a month. Sadly I can't go.
C[Thomas: No real plans.  Instead we invest(ed) in the refurbishment of our house. In fact, tomorrow we get our new kitchen.
Misawa: How exciting! Send me pictures!

C[Thomas: A vexing situation: An uncommon electric cable in the wall impeded one of four boreholes for the kitchen hood. The installation had to be stopped. I understood that a paneled wooden back wall may be the solution. We will have to discuss this with the kitchen studio.
C[Misawa: Sorry I'm slow to respond. Is it a cable that can't be moved? Most cables aren't too big a deal to move, unless they are heavy voltage.

C[Thomas: Seemingly it is the cable with heavy voltage, which comes up from the ""baking oven"". The new hood needed a bigger hole in the wall.  During the core drilling the cable and several others were torn to pieces.  All could be fixed, some moved. A friend is an electrician, but he could not offer any move. Tomorrow we will discuss it with the kitchen vendor.
C[Misawa: What a pain. Are your walls masonry or concrete? Or wood frame?
C[Thomas: With the new hole I learned that it is masonry.

Thomas: The appointment today offered a new option. We turned down a paneled wooden back wall along the whole backsplash. Now a 16 mm wooden board comes between hood and wall. It should work. The wooden board has to same color as the kitchen cabinets.
C[Misawa: Ok. Send me a picture when you have a minute. I’ll be curious to see. Does this mean the backsplash behind the stove is wood?
